description: Learn the 3 core branches of philosophy and what questions each one asks.
action: Learn the 3 core branches of philosophy and what questions each one asks.
context: Philosophy isn't one subject — it's a family of questions. Knowing the branches helps you categorize any philosophical question you encounter.
constraint_note: Read through the branches, then test yourself: given a question, which branch does it belong to?
example: "Is the death penalty moral?" → Ethics. "Do we have free will?" → Metaphysics. "How do we know anything is real?" → Epistemology.

items.reveal: What can we know? How do we know it? What counts as justified belief?
items.body: Epistemology asks: how do we know what we know? When you say 'I know the earth is round,' what makes that knowledge rather than just belief? Epistemologists distinguish between types of knowledge — knowing facts (propositional), knowing how to do things (procedural), and knowing people or places (acquaintance). The key debate: is knowledge 'justified true belief,' or is that not enough? Gettier showed in 1963 that you can have justified true belief and still be wrong by luck.

items.reveal: What is right and wrong? How should we live? What do we owe each other?
items.body: Ethics is the branch you're already doing every day without realizing it. Every time you decide whether to tell a white lie, tip a waiter, or call out unfair behavior, you're doing ethics. The field splits into three layers: meta-ethics (what IS morality?), normative ethics (what SHOULD we do?), and applied ethics (specific issues like abortion or AI). Most everyday moral arguments are normative — people disagreeing about which rules or values should guide action.

items.reveal: What exists? Do we have free will? What is consciousness?
items.body: Metaphysics tackles the questions that keep you up at 2 AM. Do you have free will, or is every choice predetermined by physics? What makes you 'you' — your body, your memories, your consciousness? Is time real or just how humans perceive change? These aren't just abstract puzzles. Your answer to 'do we have free will?' changes how you think about criminal punishment, personal responsibility, and whether praise or blame make sense.

description: Learn 6 foundational philosophers and the one idea each is most known for.
action: Learn 6 foundational philosophers and the one idea each is most known for.
context: These 6 thinkers shaped how the Western world thinks. Knowing their core idea gives you anchors for everything else.
constraint_note: Study the list, then switch to quiz mode. Can you match each philosopher to their idea?

items.reveal: The unexamined life is not worth living. Invented the Socratic method — learning through questions.
items.body: Socrates never wrote a single word — everything we know comes from his student Plato. He wandered Athens asking powerful people uncomfortable questions until they realized they didn't actually know what they claimed to know. His method was simple: keep asking 'why?' and 'what do you mean by that?' until the other person's argument collapsed. Athens eventually executed him for 'corrupting the youth,' which really meant he made important people look foolish in public.

items.reveal: Reality is divided into Forms (perfect ideals) and the physical world (imperfect copies).
items.body: Plato's big idea is wild: everything you see — this chair, that tree, your dog — is an imperfect copy of a perfect 'Form' that exists in a non-physical realm. The Form of a chair is the perfect chair-ness that all chairs participate in. His famous Cave allegory illustrates this: imagine prisoners who've only ever seen shadows on a wall. They think shadows ARE reality. Philosophy is the process of turning around and seeing the actual objects casting the shadows.

items.reveal: Virtue is the mean between extremes. Courage is between cowardice and recklessness.
items.body: Aristotle was Plato's student but disagreed with him on almost everything. Where Plato looked up to abstract ideals, Aristotle looked around at the actual world — he classified animals, studied constitutions, and analyzed poetry. His key ethical insight: virtue isn't about following rules, it's about developing good character through practice. You become brave by doing brave things, honest by practicing honesty. He essentially invented the idea that excellence is a habit, not an act.

items.reveal: I think, therefore I am. Doubted everything until he found one undeniable truth.
items.body: Descartes decided to doubt absolutely everything to find one thing he couldn't doubt. Maybe his senses were lying. Maybe the world was fake. Maybe an evil demon was tricking him. But even if everything was an illusion, the fact that he was THINKING about it proved he existed. 'Cogito ergo sum' — I think, therefore I am. This one move launched modern philosophy by making the thinking self, rather than God or nature, the starting point for knowledge.

items.reveal: Act only by rules you'd want everyone to follow (Categorical Imperative).
items.body: Kant is the philosopher who tried to save morality from being just personal opinion. His test: before you act, ask 'what if EVERYONE did this?' If you want to lie to get out of trouble, imagine a world where everyone lies — trust collapses and lying becomes pointless. The rule contradicts itself. Kant argued this test (the Categorical Imperative) gives us objective moral rules that don't depend on consequences, feelings, or culture. Critics say it's too rigid — but it's remarkably powerful.

items.reveal: God is dead — we must create our own meaning. Challenged all inherited moral systems.
items.body: Nietzsche is the most misunderstood philosopher in history. 'God is dead' wasn't a celebration — it was a warning. If traditional religion no longer provides meaning, what fills the void? Nihilism? Nationalism? Consumerism? Nietzsche's answer was radical self-creation: become who you are, create your own values, embrace life fully including its suffering. He wasn't anti-morality — he was against blindly inheriting morality without examining it. His writing style is poetic, provocative, and deliberately unsettling.

description: Learn 4 famous thought experiments and what philosophical question each one targets.
action: Learn 4 famous thought experiments and what philosophical question each one targets.
context: Thought experiments are philosophy's lab equipment. They strip away real-world noise to isolate one specific question.
constraint_note: For each, understand both the scenario AND the question it's asking.

items.reveal: Is it moral to sacrifice one to save five? Tests: do consequences justify actions?
items.body: A runaway trolley is heading toward five people. You can pull a lever to divert it to a side track where it will kill one person instead. Most people say: pull the lever. But now imagine you're on a bridge and can push a large stranger onto the tracks to stop the trolley — same math, one dies to save five. Most people refuse. This inconsistency is the whole point: it reveals that we don't just care about outcomes. Something about the ACT of pushing feels different from pulling a lever.

items.reveal: How do you know you're not a brain being fed fake experiences? Tests: can we trust our senses?
items.body: Imagine scientists removed your brain, put it in a jar, and connected it to a computer feeding you perfectly realistic fake experiences. You'd feel, see, and hear everything as normal. How would you know? You couldn't. This isn't just sci-fi — it's a serious challenge to the idea that our senses give us reliable knowledge about reality. The Matrix borrowed this idea directly. Descartes raised the same worry in the 1600s with his 'evil demon' scenario.

items.reveal: If you replace every plank of a ship, is it the same ship? Tests: what makes identity persist?
items.body: An ancient ship is preserved in a museum. Over centuries, every rotting plank is replaced with new wood. Eventually no original material remains. Is it still the same ship? Now imagine someone collected all the old planks and rebuilt the original. Which one is the 'real' Ship of Theseus? This puzzle applies to you: your body replaces most of its cells every 7-15 years. Are you the same person you were at age 10? What makes identity persist through change?

items.reveal: Following rules to produce Chinese without understanding it — is that thinking? Tests: can machines think?
items.body: Imagine you're locked in a room with a rulebook. Chinese characters are slid under the door. You follow the rules to produce Chinese responses and slide them back. To people outside, it looks like you understand Chinese — but you don't understand a word. You're just following rules mechanically. Searle argued this is exactly what computers do: they manipulate symbols without understanding meaning. It's the strongest argument against the idea that AI can truly 'think.'

description: Learn 5 major schools of thought and their core claim about how to live.
action: Learn 5 major schools of thought and their core claim about how to live.
context: Each school offers a different answer to "what makes a good life?" Knowing them gives you a menu of life philosophies to draw from.
constraint_note: Focus on the one-sentence core claim. That's what distinguishes each school.

items.reveal: Focus only on what you can control. Accept what you can't. Virtue is the only true good.
items.body: Stoicism isn't about suppressing emotions — that's a common misconception. It's about recognizing what's in your control (your reactions, choices, effort) and what isn't (other people, weather, the economy). Marcus Aurelius, a Roman emperor, practiced Stoicism while running an empire and fighting wars. His private journal, Meditations, is basically a self-help book written 2,000 years ago. The core practice: when something upsets you, ask 'is this within my control?' If not, let it go.

items.reveal: Pleasure is the highest good — but true pleasure is simple: friendship, freedom, thought. Not hedonism.
items.body: Epicurus gets a bad rap. People think Epicureanism means 'eat, drink, and be merry' — but Epicurus actually lived on bread and water. His point was that the SIMPLEST pleasures are the best: a good conversation with friends, freedom from anxiety, a quiet afternoon thinking. He argued that chasing luxury and status creates more pain than pleasure. His school, the Garden, was one of the first to admit women and enslaved people as equal members — radical for 300 BC.

items.reveal: Existence precedes essence. You're not born with a purpose — you must create your own meaning.
items.body: Existentialism starts with a terrifying idea: there's no script for your life. You weren't born with a purpose, a destiny, or a nature that determines who you should be. You exist first, then you define yourself through your choices. Sartre captured this as 'existence precedes essence.' The flip side of this freedom is total responsibility — you can't blame your parents, your culture, or your circumstances. Your life is your project, and you're the only author.

items.reveal: Truth is what works. An idea is true if it produces useful, practical consequences.
items.body: Pragmatism is the most American philosophy — literally invented in the US by Charles Peirce, William James, and John Dewey. Its core idea: stop asking 'is this TRUE in some abstract sense?' and start asking 'does this WORK?' If believing in free will makes you take responsibility for your life, then free will is 'true enough.' James applied this to religion: if belief in God makes someone's life better and more moral, that belief has practical truth. Critics call this intellectually lazy; fans call it refreshingly honest.

items.reveal: Life has no inherent meaning, but we must live fully anyway. "One must imagine Sisyphus happy."
items.body: Albert Camus looked at the universe and saw no inherent meaning — no God, no cosmic plan, no purpose written into reality. But instead of despair (nihilism) or inventing meaning (existentialism), he proposed a third option: acknowledge the absurdity AND live fully anyway. His symbol is Sisyphus, condemned to push a boulder uphill forever, watching it roll back down each time. Camus says: 'One must imagine Sisyphus happy.' The rebellion against meaninglessness IS the meaning.

description: Test your knowledge of key philosophers. Complete each statement from memory.
action: Test your knowledge of key philosophers. Complete each statement from memory.
context: Active recall is how knowledge sticks. If you get one wrong, that's the most valuable learning moment.
constraint_note: Try to answer from memory before revealing.

items.prompt: Socrates' method of teaching through questions is called the ___ method.
Plato believed the physical world is an imperfect copy of perfect ___.
Descartes' famous statement "I think, therefore I ___" proved his own existence.
items.hint: The one thing he couldn't doubt

items.prompt: Kant's ___ Imperative says: act only by rules you'd want everyone to follow.

description: Learn the basic building blocks of philosophical arguments: premises, conclusions, deduction, and induction.
action: Learn the basic building blocks of philosophical arguments: premises, conclusions, deduction, and induction.
context: Every philosophical argument has the same structure: premises that lead to a conclusion. Understanding this structure lets you evaluate any argument.
constraint_note: Focus on the difference between deduction (certain) and induction (probable).
example: Deduction: "All humans are mortal. Socrates is human. Therefore Socrates is mortal." — the conclusion MUST follow. Induction: "The sun rose every day so far, so it will rise tomorrow." — probable but not certain.

items.reveal: A statement assumed to be true that supports the conclusion. Arguments have one or more premises.
items.body: A premise is any statement you're treating as true to support your conclusion. 'All humans are mortal' is a premise. 'Socrates is human' is another premise. Together they support the conclusion 'Socrates is mortal.' The tricky part: premises can be unstated. When someone says 'she's a doctor, so she must be smart,' the hidden premise is 'all doctors are smart' — which is debatable. Finding hidden premises is one of the most useful critical thinking skills you can develop.

items.reveal: The statement the argument is trying to prove. It should follow logically from the premises.
items.body: The conclusion is what the argument is trying to prove — the 'therefore' statement. It should follow logically from the premises. A common mistake: people state conclusions as if they're obvious without providing premises. 'We should ban social media for kids' is a conclusion. Without premises (why? what evidence?), it's just an assertion. Tip: look for signal words like 'therefore,' 'so,' 'thus,' 'it follows that' — they usually introduce the conclusion.

items.reveal: If premises are true, conclusion MUST be true. Goes from general rules to specific cases.
items.body: Deductive reasoning gives you certainty — IF the premises are true, the conclusion MUST be true. 'All mammals breathe air. Whales are mammals. Therefore whales breathe air.' There's no wiggle room. The downside: deduction can only rearrange what you already know. It can't discover new facts about the world. That's why science relies more on induction. Deduction is the backbone of mathematics, formal logic, and legal reasoning where certainty matters.

items.reveal: Premises make conclusion LIKELY but not certain. Goes from specific observations to general rules.
items.body: Inductive reasoning is how you navigate daily life. You've seen the sun rise every morning, so you conclude it will rise tomorrow. You've met five friendly dogs, so you assume the next dog will be friendly. Induction gives you probability, not certainty — and sometimes it fails spectacularly. Europeans believed all swans were white until they discovered black swans in Australia. David Hume pointed out that no amount of past observations can logically guarantee future ones. This is called the 'problem of induction.'

description: Learn the 3 positions on free will: determinism, libertarian free will, and compatibilism.
action: Learn the 3 positions on free will: determinism, libertarian free will, and compatibilism.
context: The free will debate is one of philosophy's oldest. Your position on it affects how you think about responsibility, punishment, and praise.
constraint_note: Each position answers: "Could you have done otherwise?" differently.

items.reveal: Every event is caused by prior events. Your "choices" are the inevitable result of physics + history. Free will is an illusion.
items.body: Hard determinism says your feeling of choosing is an illusion. Every thought you have, every 'decision' you make, was determined by prior causes stretching back to the Big Bang. Your brain is made of atoms following physical laws — where's the room for free choice? Neuroscientist Benjamin Libet found that brain activity for a decision begins BEFORE you're consciously aware of deciding. If your brain decides before 'you' do, who's really in charge? This view has radical implications for criminal justice — can you punish someone for something they couldn't have avoided?

items.reveal: Humans have genuine ability to choose otherwise. Something about consciousness escapes causal chains.
items.body: Not the political kind — philosophical libertarianism about free will says humans genuinely can choose otherwise. Something about consciousness or the will escapes the causal chain of physics. Philosopher Robert Kane argues that at key moments of inner conflict, the outcome is genuinely undetermined — you could go either way. Critics ask: if your choice isn't determined by your character, reasons, or desires, then isn't it just random? And random isn't the same as free. This is the libertarian's biggest challenge.

items.reveal: Free will and determinism are compatible. You're "free" if you act on your own desires, even if those desires were determined.
items.body: Compatibilism is the most popular position among professional philosophers. It says: yes, your choices are caused by prior events (your desires, beliefs, character). But that's not a problem — that's what free will IS. You're 'free' when you act on your own desires without external coercion. A prisoner forced to work isn't free. A person who chooses to work because they love it IS free — even if that love was shaped by their upbringing. The key distinction: being caused is not the same as being coerced.

description: Test your knowledge of philosophical schools. Fill in the blanks.
action: Test your knowledge of philosophical schools. Fill in the blanks.
context: Can you recall the core claim of each school without looking?
constraint_note: Answer from memory first.

items.prompt: Stoicism teaches: focus only on what you can ___.
Existentialism says: ___ precedes essence.
items.hint: Sartre's famous claim — you exist first, then define yourself

items.prompt: Camus said "One must imagine ___ happy" to illustrate absurdism.
items.hint: The Greek figure who pushes a boulder uphill forever

items.prompt: Epicurus taught that true pleasure comes from simple things: friendship, freedom, and ___.

description: Learn the debate between Empiricism and Rationalism — where does knowledge come from?
action: Learn the debate between Empiricism and Rationalism — where does knowledge come from?
context: This is epistemology's central question. Every time you argue about evidence vs. logic, you're replaying this debate.
constraint_note: The key question: can you know things WITHOUT experience, or does all knowledge require it?

items.reveal: All knowledge comes from sensory experience. The mind starts as a blank slate (tabula rasa).
items.body: Empiricists say the mind starts as a blank slate — John Locke's famous 'tabula rasa.' Everything you know, you learned through your five senses. See enough sunrises and you learn the sun rises daily. Taste enough lemons and you learn they're sour. David Hume took this further: even cause and effect is just a habit of observation. You see billiard ball A hit ball B, and B moves. But you never actually SEE the 'causing' — you just see one event followed by another. Empiricism is the philosophical foundation of modern science.

items.reveal: Some knowledge is innate or discoverable through reason alone, without needing experience.
items.body: Rationalists argue some knowledge is built into the mind or discoverable through pure reason. Consider mathematics: you don't need to observe anything to know that 2+2=4 or that a triangle's angles sum to 180°. Descartes argued that the idea of God, the concept of infinity, and basic logical truths are innate — no amount of sensory experience could produce them. Leibniz pointed out that necessary truths (like math) can't come from experience, because experience only shows what IS, not what MUST be.

items.reveal: Knowledge that doesn't require experience. Example: 2+2=4. You don't need to observe this.
items.body: A priori knowledge is what you can know without any experience at all. 'All bachelors are unmarried' — you don't need to survey bachelors to know this. It's true by definition. Mathematical truths are the clearest examples: 7+5=12 is true regardless of what you observe. Kant argued that even the structure of space and time is a priori — your mind imposes spatial and temporal order on experience, rather than learning it from experience. This was his revolutionary 'Copernican turn' in philosophy.

items.reveal: Knowledge that requires experience. Example: "Water boils at 100°C." You must observe this.
items.body: A posteriori knowledge requires experience — you have to go out and look. 'Water boils at 100°C at sea level' is a posteriori: someone had to actually heat water and measure. 'There are eight planets in our solar system' required telescopes and observation. The interesting cases are borderline: is 'nothing can be red and green all over at the same time' a priori (knowable by logic) or a posteriori (you need to see colors to know this)? This boundary is still debated.

description: Learn the Social Contract theory: why do we have governments? Three thinkers, three answers.
action: Learn the Social Contract theory: why do we have governments? Three thinkers, three answers.
context: Every political argument about rights, laws, and government power traces back to social contract theory.
constraint_note: Each thinker starts from a different view of human nature, which leads to a different conclusion about government.

items.reveal: Without government, life is "nasty, brutish, and short." We need a strong ruler to prevent chaos.
items.body: Thomas Hobbes lived through the English Civil War and it shaped everything he wrote. He imagined life without government — the 'state of nature' — and concluded it would be a war of all against all. Without laws, anyone could kill you for your stuff. His solution: everyone surrenders their freedom to an absolute sovereign (a king or assembly) who maintains order through force. Yes, you lose liberty — but you gain security. Hobbes essentially argued that even a bad government is better than no government.

items.reveal: People have natural rights (life, liberty, property). Government exists to protect them — and can be overthrown if it fails.
items.body: John Locke had a much sunnier view of human nature than Hobbes. He believed people in the state of nature would mostly get along, respecting each other's natural rights to life, liberty, and property. Government exists to PROTECT these pre-existing rights, not to grant them. And here's the revolutionary part: if a government violates the rights it was created to protect, the people have the right to overthrow it. This idea directly inspired the American Declaration of Independence and the French Revolution.

items.reveal: Humans are naturally good but corrupted by society. Government should express the "general will" of the people.
items.body: Rousseau flipped the script entirely. He argued that humans in the state of nature were peaceful, compassionate, and free — it was CIVILIZATION that corrupted them. Private property created inequality, cities created competition, and social hierarchies created envy. His solution wasn't to go back to nature but to create a government based on the 'general will' — what the community as a whole truly wants, not just what the majority votes for. This distinction between popular vote and general will is still debated in democratic theory.

description: Compare Empiricism vs Rationalism side by side. What does each say about the source of knowledge?
action: Compare Empiricism vs Rationalism side by side. What does each say about the source of knowledge?
context: These two traditions defined modern philosophy. Most thinkers fall somewhere on this spectrum.
constraint_note: For each pair, think about which side you lean toward and why.

pairs.left: Empiricism: Mind starts as blank slate
pairs.right: Rationalism: Mind has innate ideas

pairs.left: Knowledge comes FROM experience
pairs.right: Knowledge comes THROUGH reason

pairs.left: Science is the model: observe, test
pairs.right: Mathematics is the model: deduce, prove

pairs.left: Weakness: can't prove universal laws from observations
pairs.right: Weakness: reason alone can lead to unfounded conclusions

description: Compare Plato vs Aristotle — teacher and student who disagreed on almost everything.
action: Compare Plato vs Aristotle — teacher and student who disagreed on almost everything.
context: This is the foundational split in Western philosophy. Plato looks up (to ideals), Aristotle looks around (at the world).
constraint_note: Notice how their disagreement echoes in modern debates: theory vs practice, idealism vs realism.

pairs.left: Plato: True reality is the world of Forms
pairs.right: Aristotle: True reality is the physical world we observe

pairs.left: Knowledge through reason and contemplation
pairs.right: Knowledge through observation and classification

pairs.left: The ideal state is ruled by philosopher-kings
pairs.right: The best state balances different forms of government

pairs.left: Art is a dangerous imitation of imitations
pairs.right: Art is valuable — it lets us experience catharsis

description: Practice the Socratic method. Pick any belief you hold and ask yourself 5 "why" questions in sequence.
action: Practice the Socratic method. Pick any belief you hold and ask yourself 5 "why" questions in sequence.
context: Socrates never told people what to think — he asked questions until they discovered contradictions in their own beliefs. This is the most powerful thinking tool in philosophy.
constraint_note: Write your belief, then 5 why-questions and your honest answers. Don't defend — explore.
example: Belief: "Hard work leads to success." Why? → Because effort produces results. Why do you believe that? → I've seen it in my life. Is that always true? → No, some people work hard and fail. So what else matters? → Luck, connections, timing. Then is your original belief accurate? → It's incomplete...

description: Test your understanding of social contract theory. Who said what?
action: Test your understanding of social contract theory. Who said what?
context: These three thinkers gave very different answers to "why government?" based on different views of human nature.
constraint_note: Think about each thinker's view of human nature to derive the answer.

items.prompt: ___ believed life without government would be "nasty, brutish, and short."

items.hint: Influenced the American Declaration of Independence

items.hint: The romantic — civilization is the problem

items.hint: All three thinkers contributed to this idea

description: The free will question: write your current position in 3-4 sentences. Are your choices truly free?
action: The free will question: write your current position in 3-4 sentences. Are your choices truly free?
context: This isn't about getting the "right" answer — philosophers have debated this for millennia. It's about articulating YOUR position clearly.
constraint_note: State your position, give one reason for it, and acknowledge the strongest objection against it.
example: Example: "I lean toward compatibilism. My choices feel free because they come from my own desires, even if those desires were shaped by genetics and environment. The strongest objection is that this redefines 'free' to mean something weaker than what most people mean by it."

description: Learn Phenomenology — the philosophy of conscious experience. What is it like to BE you?
action: Learn Phenomenology — the philosophy of conscious experience. What is it like to BE you?
context: Phenomenology asks: before we theorize about the world, what is the raw experience of being conscious? It's the foundation of modern philosophy of mind.
constraint_note: The key insight: we never experience "raw reality" — we always experience reality AS something (as threatening, as beautiful, as boring).

items.reveal: Philosophy of how things appear to consciousness. Not what IS real, but how we EXPERIENCE reality.
items.body: Phenomenology is the study of how things APPEAR to consciousness, not how they 'really are.' When you see a red apple, a physicist describes wavelengths of light. A phenomenologist asks: what is the EXPERIENCE of seeing red actually like? This 'what it's like' quality of experience is what philosophers call 'qualia.' Phenomenology became hugely influential in psychology, psychiatry, and AI research — because understanding consciousness requires understanding experience from the inside, not just measuring brain activity.

items.reveal: Proposed "bracketing" — setting aside assumptions about whether things are real to study pure experience.
items.body: Edmund Husserl wanted philosophy to be as rigorous as mathematics. His method: 'bracket' all your assumptions about whether things are real, and just describe what appears in consciousness. Don't ask 'does this table exist?' — ask 'what is my experience of this table?' This sounds strange, but it's powerful. By stripping away theories and assumptions, you get to the raw structure of experience. Husserl called this the 'phenomenological reduction' — reducing experience to its essential features.

items.reveal: We don't observe the world from outside — we're always already embedded in it. Understanding comes from living, not theorizing.
items.body: Martin Heidegger, Husserl's student, took phenomenology in a radical direction. He argued we don't experience the world as detached observers — we're always already IN it, using things, caring about outcomes, projecting into the future. You don't see a hammer as 'an object with properties' — you see it as something-for-hammering. This 'being-in-the-world' means understanding can't be separated from living. Heidegger's work is notoriously difficult to read but profoundly influenced existentialism, hermeneutics, and postmodernism.

items.reveal: Consciousness is always consciousness OF something. You can't just "be aware" — you're always aware of something specific.
items.body: Intentionality doesn't mean 'doing something on purpose.' In philosophy, it means consciousness is always ABOUT something — always directed at an object. You can't just 'be afraid' — you're afraid OF something. You can't just 'believe' — you believe THAT something. Even imagination is intentional: you imagine SOMETHING. This seems obvious, but it has deep implications. It means consciousness isn't a container that holds thoughts — it's more like a spotlight that's always pointing at something. There's no 'empty' consciousness.

description: Learn Hegel's Dialectic — how ideas evolve through contradiction and resolution.
action: Learn Hegel's Dialectic — how ideas evolve through contradiction and resolution.
context: The dialectic explains how human thought progresses: every idea generates its opposite, and the tension produces something new. It's how science, politics, and culture actually evolve.
constraint_note: Follow the steps, then try to apply the pattern to a real example.
example: Example: Thesis: "Individual freedom is the highest value." Antithesis: "But unlimited freedom leads to exploitation." Synthesis: "Regulated freedom — rights with responsibilities."

steps: Start with a THESIS — an idea or position that seems true.
Identify the ANTITHESIS — the contradiction or opposite that the thesis generates.
The tension between thesis and antithesis produces a SYNTHESIS — a new idea that incorporates both.
The synthesis then becomes a new thesis, and the cycle continues.
This is how Hegel believed all of history progresses — through contradiction and resolution.

description: Compare Eastern and Western philosophical traditions. What does each prioritize? Write 4-5 sentences.
action: Compare Eastern and Western philosophical traditions. What does each prioritize? Write 4-5 sentences.
context: Most philosophy education is Western-centric. Recognizing the Eastern tradition reveals blind spots in how you think about self, reality, and ethics.
constraint_note: Don't just list differences — identify what each tradition ASSUMES that the other questions.
example: Western philosophy tends to assume a separate self that reasons about an external world. Eastern traditions (Buddhism, Taoism, Hinduism) often question whether the self is real at all, and whether separation from the world is an illusion.

description: The Hard Problem of Consciousness: why does it FEEL like something to be you? Write your take in 3-4 sentences.
action: The Hard Problem of Consciousness: why does it FEEL like something to be you? Write your take in 3-4 sentences.
context: David Chalmers distinguished the "easy problems" (how the brain processes information) from the "hard problem" (why there's subjective experience at all). Science can explain brain activity but not why it feels like something.
constraint_note: State whether you think science will eventually explain consciousness, and why or why not.

description: Three responses to "life has no inherent meaning": nihilism, existentialism, absurdism. Which do you find most compelling? Argue for it in 4-5 sentences.
action: Three responses to "life has no inherent meaning": nihilism, existentialism, absurdism. Which do you find most compelling? Argue for it in 4-5 sentences.
context: This is philosophy's biggest question. Nihilism says: there's no meaning, period. Existentialism says: create your own. Absurdism says: there's no meaning, but live fully anyway.
constraint_note: Don't just state your preference — argue for it. Why is your chosen response better than the other two?

description: Learn the 3 major ethical frameworks and how each one decides what's "right."
action: Learn the 3 major ethical frameworks and how each one decides what's "right."
context: Most moral disagreements come down to people using different frameworks without realizing it.
constraint_note: Focus on the core question each framework asks. That's the key difference.

items.reveal: Judge actions by outcomes. The right action produces the most happiness for the most people.
items.body: Utilitarianism is beautifully simple: the right action is whatever produces the most happiness for the most people. Invented by Jeremy Bentham in the 1700s, it was revolutionary because it treated everyone's happiness as equally important — a king's pleasure counts no more than a peasant's. The appeal is obvious: it gives you a clear decision procedure. The problem is also obvious: it could justify horrifying things (torturing one person to make a million people slightly happier) if the math works out.

items.reveal: Judge actions by rules/duties. Some actions are wrong regardless of outcomes (e.g., lying is always wrong).
items.body: Deontology says some actions are right or wrong regardless of their consequences. Lying is wrong even if it produces good outcomes. Keeping promises is right even when breaking them would help more people. Kant's version is the most famous: act only by rules you'd want EVERYONE to follow. The strength is that it protects individual rights absolutely — you can't sacrifice one person for the greater good. The weakness: what do you do when two duties conflict? 'Don't lie' vs 'protect the innocent' — Kant struggled with this.

items.reveal: Judge actions by character. A good person acts with courage, honesty, compassion — not by calculating.
items.body: Virtue ethics doesn't ask 'what should I DO?' — it asks 'what kind of person should I BE?' Instead of following rules or calculating outcomes, you develop good character traits (virtues) through practice. Aristotle identified virtues like courage, honesty, generosity, and justice. Each virtue is a balance: courage is between cowardice and recklessness. The appeal: it matches how we actually think about morality. We admire people for their character, not for being good calculators. The challenge: it doesn't give clear answers to specific dilemmas.

description: Test your knowledge of ethical frameworks. Complete each statement.
action: Test your knowledge of ethical frameworks. Complete each statement.
context: Active recall is how knowledge sticks.
constraint_note: Try to answer from memory before revealing.

items.prompt: A utilitarian asks: "Which action produces the greatest ___?"
items.hint: Think: outcomes for the most people

items.prompt: A deontologist asks: "Does this action follow a universal ___?"
A virtue ethicist asks: "What would a ___ person do?"
Lying to save a life is justified by ___ but condemned by ___.
items.hint: One cares about outcomes, the other about rules

description: Learn Kant's Categorical Imperative — the most famous rule-based ethical test.
action: Learn Kant's Categorical Imperative — the most famous rule-based ethical test.
context: Kant wanted a single test that could determine if ANY action is moral. The Categorical Imperative is his answer.
constraint_note: The key question: "What if everyone did this?" If the answer leads to contradiction, the action is wrong.
example: "Should I lie to get out of trouble?" → If everyone lied, trust would collapse and lying would become pointless. Contradiction. Therefore lying is always wrong.

items.reveal: Act only according to rules you could will to be universal laws. "What if everyone did this?"
items.body: This is Kant's most famous test: before you act, ask 'what if everyone did this?' Want to lie to get out of trouble? Imagine a world where everyone lies — trust collapses, and lying becomes pointless because nobody believes anything. The rule contradicts itself when universalized. Want to break a promise? If everyone broke promises, the concept of promising would cease to exist. The test works by finding logical contradictions, not by predicting consequences. That's what makes it different from utilitarianism.

items.reveal: Never treat people merely as means to an end. Always also treat them as ends in themselves.
items.body: Kant's second formulation hits differently: never treat people MERELY as means to your end. You can hire someone (using them as a means) as long as you also respect their dignity, pay them fairly, and treat them as a person with their own goals. But manipulating someone, deceiving them, or exploiting their vulnerability treats them as a tool — that's always wrong. This principle is the philosophical foundation of human rights, informed consent in medicine, and labor protections. It says every person has inherent worth that can't be traded away.

items.reveal: Act as if you're making laws for a community where everyone is both ruler and subject.
items.body: The third formulation asks you to imagine a community where everyone is both a lawmaker and a subject. Every rule you propose, you must also follow. Every person affected by the rule has a voice. This is Kant's vision of an ideal moral community — not a democracy exactly, but a community of mutual respect where no one is above the rules. It's remarkably close to John Rawls's 'veil of ignorance' idea 200 years later. The practical takeaway: don't make exceptions for yourself.

description: Study the Trolley Problem and its variants. What do your intuitions reveal about your ethics?
action: Study the Trolley Problem and its variants. What do your intuitions reveal about your ethics?
context: The trolley problem isn't about trolleys — it's about whether you judge morality by outcomes or by actions.
constraint_note: Notice how your intuition CHANGES between variants. That inconsistency is the whole point.

items.reveal: Trolley heading toward 5 people. Pull lever to divert to track with 1 person. Most people say: pull it.
items.body: Philippa Foot introduced this in 1967, and it became the most discussed thought experiment in modern ethics. The setup is simple: trolley heading toward five people, you can pull a lever to divert it to a track with one person. About 90% of people say pull the lever. The reasoning seems straightforward: five lives outweigh one. But this intuition — that you should actively cause one death to prevent five — has surprising implications. It suggests most people are at least somewhat utilitarian in their moral thinking.

items.reveal: Push a large man off a bridge to stop the trolley and save 5. Same math (1 vs 5). Most people say: don't push.
items.body: Judith Jarvis Thomson added this variant: you're on a bridge above the tracks. A large man is next to you. If you push him onto the tracks, his body will stop the trolley, saving five people. Same math as before — one death to save five. But about 90% of people say DON'T push. Why? The physical act of pushing someone to their death feels fundamentally different from pulling a lever, even though the outcome is identical. This gap between our moral intuitions and utilitarian logic is what makes the trolley problem so powerful.

items.reveal: If outcomes are all that matter, both cases are identical. But most people feel pushing is worse. This suggests we care about more than consequences.
items.body: The puzzle isn't about trolleys — it's about YOU. If you'd pull the lever but not push the man, your moral reasoning is inconsistent. A pure utilitarian would do both (same outcome). A pure deontologist might do neither (you're causing a death either way). But most people split: lever yes, push no. This suggests our moral psychology uses MULTIPLE frameworks simultaneously — we care about outcomes AND about the nature of our actions AND about physical directness. Understanding this inconsistency is the first step toward more reflective moral thinking.

items.reveal: If you'd pull but not push: you're not a pure utilitarian. You have deontological intuitions about using people as means.
items.body: The trolley problem has real-world applications that go far beyond philosophy classrooms. Self-driving car programmers must decide: should the car swerve to save passengers at the risk of hitting pedestrians? Doctors face triage decisions: which patient gets the last ventilator? Military commanders weigh civilian casualties against strategic objectives. In each case, the trolley problem's core tension — between maximizing lives saved and respecting individual rights — is exactly what decision-makers face.

description: Learn the debate: is morality objective (universal) or relative (cultural)?
action: Learn the debate: is morality objective (universal) or relative (cultural)?
context: This is the first question in ethics. Your answer shapes everything else — if morality is relative, there's no basis to criticize any culture's practices.
constraint_note: Consider: if morality is purely cultural, can we say slavery was wrong even when a culture accepted it?

items.reveal: Some moral truths hold regardless of culture or opinion. Murder is wrong everywhere, not just where people think so.
items.body: Moral objectivism says some things are wrong regardless of what anyone thinks. Slavery was wrong in ancient Rome even though Romans accepted it. The Holocaust was wrong even though Nazi ideology endorsed it. The appeal is powerful: without objective morality, you can't criticize any culture's practices. But the challenge is equally powerful: WHERE do these objective moral truths come from? God? Human nature? Reason? Each answer has problems. And how do you explain the massive moral disagreement across cultures if there's one objective truth?

items.reveal: Morality is determined by cultural norms. What's "right" in one society may be "wrong" in another. No universal standard.
items.body: Moral relativism says morality is determined by cultural norms — there's no view from nowhere, no culture-independent standard. What's 'right' in one society may be 'wrong' in another, and neither is objectively correct. This sounds tolerant, but it has a dark side: if morality is purely cultural, you can't say slavery was wrong in cultures that accepted it. You can't criticize female genital mutilation in cultures that practice it. Most people who call themselves relativists don't actually accept these implications.

items.reveal: Different cultures DO have different moral beliefs (factual observation). Doesn't necessarily mean all are equally valid.
items.body: Cultural relativism is often confused with moral relativism, but it's actually a descriptive claim, not a normative one. It simply observes: different cultures DO have different moral beliefs. Some cultures practice polygamy, others don't. Some consider eating insects normal, others find it disgusting. This is just a fact about the world. The philosophical leap — from 'cultures disagree about morality' to 'therefore no culture is right' — is called the 'diversity argument,' and most philosophers consider it a logical fallacy. Disagreement doesn't prove there's no truth.

description: Learn the concept of rights: negative rights (freedoms FROM) vs positive rights (entitlements TO).
action: Learn the concept of rights: negative rights (freedoms FROM) vs positive rights (entitlements TO).
context: Every political debate about healthcare, education, or gun control is really a debate about what kind of rights people have.
constraint_note: The key distinction: negative rights require others to leave you alone; positive rights require others to provide something.

items.reveal: Right to be left alone. Free speech, freedom from assault, property rights. Others must NOT interfere.
items.body: Negative rights are 'leave me alone' rights. Freedom of speech means the government can't stop you from speaking — it doesn't mean anyone has to give you a microphone or an audience. The right to life means nobody can kill you — it doesn't mean someone must feed you. These rights are 'negative' because they require others to NOT do something. Libertarians tend to emphasize negative rights because they require minimal government: just prevent people from harming each other, and otherwise stay out of the way.

items.reveal: Right to receive something. Healthcare, education, housing. Others (or government) MUST provide.
items.body: Positive rights are 'give me something' rights. The right to education means someone must provide schools and teachers. The right to healthcare means someone must provide medical treatment. These rights require active provision, which means someone has to pay for them — usually through taxes. This is where political disagreements get heated: if healthcare is a right, can the government force doctors to work? Can it force taxpayers to fund it? Positive rights create obligations on others in a way negative rights don't.

items.reveal: Rights you have simply by being human — life, liberty, property. Not granted by government, only protected by it.
items.body: John Locke argued that certain rights exist BEFORE any government — life, liberty, and property. You have them simply by being human, not because any law grants them. Government's job is to PROTECT these pre-existing rights, not to create them. This idea was revolutionary: it meant a government that violates natural rights is illegitimate, and people can overthrow it. Thomas Jefferson borrowed this directly for the Declaration of Independence: 'We hold these truths to be self-evident, that all men are created equal, endowed with certain unalienable rights.'

description: Test your ethics knowledge. Fill in the blanks.
action: Test your ethics knowledge. Fill in the blanks.
context: These are foundational concepts — make sure you can recall them without prompts.
constraint_note: Answer from memory first.

items.prompt: Kant's test for morality: "What if ___ did this?"
The trolley problem tests whether you judge morality by ___ or by ___.
Moral ___ says there are no universal moral truths — morality depends on culture.
___ rights require others to leave you alone; ___ rights require others to provide something.

description: Learn how a utilitarian actually calculates the "right" action using Bentham's method.
action: Learn how a utilitarian actually calculates the "right" action using Bentham's method.
context: Utilitarianism sounds simple ("maximize happiness") but applying it requires a systematic approach.
constraint_note: Follow the steps, then think about the limitations of this approach.

steps: Identify all available actions you could take.
For each action, list everyone who would be affected.
Estimate the pleasure and pain each person would experience (intensity, duration, certainty).
Sum up total pleasure minus total pain for each action.
Choose the action with the highest net happiness.
Problem: can you really quantify happiness? Can you predict all consequences? This is utilitarianism's biggest weakness.

description: Learn Aristotle's Virtue Ethics in depth: eudaimonia, the golden mean, and the virtues.
action: Learn Aristotle's Virtue Ethics in depth: eudaimonia, the golden mean, and the virtues.
context: Virtue ethics doesn't ask "what should I do?" but "what kind of person should I be?" It's about character, not rules or calculations.
constraint_note: The golden mean is the key insight: every virtue is a balance between two extremes.
example: Courage is the mean between cowardice (too little) and recklessness (too much). Generosity is between stinginess and wastefulness.

items.reveal: Often translated as "happiness" but really means "flourishing" — living well and doing well over a whole life.
items.body: Eudaimonia is often translated as 'happiness,' but that's misleading. It's closer to 'flourishing' or 'living well and doing well.' Aristotle didn't mean feeling good — he meant living a complete, excellent human life. A person with eudaimonia has good character, meaningful relationships, engages in worthwhile activities, and has enough external goods (health, friends, some wealth) to exercise their virtues. You can't achieve eudaimonia in a single moment — it's a judgment about your whole life, like saying 'she lived well.'

items.reveal: Every virtue is the balance point between two vices: one of excess, one of deficiency.
items.body: The golden mean is Aristotle's insight that every virtue sits between two vices — one of excess and one of deficiency. Courage is between cowardice (too little) and recklessness (too much). Generosity is between stinginess and wastefulness. Honesty is between secretiveness and oversharing. The mean isn't a mathematical midpoint — it depends on the situation. A soldier needs more courage than an accountant. Finding the right balance in each specific context is what Aristotle called practical wisdom.

items.reveal: The master virtue — knowing HOW to find the mean in each specific situation. Can't be taught by rules, only by experience.
items.body: Phronesis (practical wisdom) is the master virtue in Aristotle's system — without it, you can't exercise any other virtue well. It's the ability to perceive what a situation requires and respond appropriately. Rules can't capture it: 'always be honest' doesn't tell you HOW honest to be with a grieving friend. Practical wisdom is knowing when to speak and when to stay silent, when to be firm and when to be gentle. You develop it through experience, mentorship, and reflection — not from reading a textbook. It's why Aristotle said ethics can't be learned by the young.

items.reveal: You become courageous by practicing courage. Virtues are habits, not decisions. Character is built through repeated action.
items.body: Aristotle's most practical insight: you become virtuous by PRACTICING virtue, not by thinking about it. You don't become courageous by reading about courage — you become courageous by doing courageous things, starting small. Each act of courage makes the next one easier until it becomes second nature. This is remarkably similar to modern psychology's understanding of habit formation. It also means character education matters: the habits you form in childhood shape who you become. Aristotle would have loved the '10,000 hours' concept.

description: Apply all 3 ethical frameworks to the trolley problem. What does each say, and which do you agree with?
action: Apply all 3 ethical frameworks to the trolley problem. What does each say, and which do you agree with?
context: This forces you to think FROM each framework, not just ABOUT them. That's the difference between knowing philosophy and doing philosophy.
constraint_note: Write 1-2 sentences per framework, then state your own position with a reason.
example: Utilitarian: pull the lever — 5 lives saved outweigh 1. Deontologist: don't pull — you'd be using the 1 person as a means. Virtue ethicist: what would a compassionate, courageous person do in this moment?

description: Dilemma: Your friend asks if their terrible business idea is good. Honest feedback would crush them. What do you do? Analyze using 2 frameworks.
action: Dilemma: Your friend asks if their terrible business idea is good. Honest feedback would crush them. What do you do? Analyze using 2 frameworks.
context: Real moral dilemmas rarely have clean answers. The value is in seeing how different frameworks pull you in different directions.
constraint_note: Apply utilitarianism AND deontology. Show how they give different advice. Then state what you'd actually do.

description: Rawls' Veil of Ignorance: if you didn't know your position in society (rich/poor, race, gender), what rules would you create? Write 3 rules.
action: Rawls' Veil of Ignorance: if you didn't know your position in society (rich/poor, race, gender), what rules would you create? Write 3 rules.
context: Rawls argued that fair rules are ones you'd choose if you didn't know where you'd end up. This removes self-interest from moral reasoning.
constraint_note: Imagine you could be ANYONE in the society you're designing. What 3 rules would protect you no matter where you landed?

description: Moral luck: two drunk drivers take the same risk. One gets home safe, the other kills a pedestrian. Should they be punished differently? Write your argument.
action: Moral luck: two drunk drivers take the same risk. One gets home safe, the other kills a pedestrian. Should they be punished differently? Write your argument.
context: Thomas Nagel identified "moral luck" — we judge people differently based on outcomes they didn't control. This challenges our entire concept of moral responsibility.
constraint_note: State your position clearly, give your strongest reason, and address the best counterargument.

description: Test your deeper ethics knowledge.
action: Test your deeper ethics knowledge.
context: These concepts require understanding, not just memorization.
constraint_note: Think through each answer carefully.

items.prompt: Rawls' ___ of ___ asks you to design society without knowing your position in it.
Aristotle called the goal of a good life ___, meaning "flourishing" not just "happiness."
The ___ ___ says every virtue is a balance between two extremes.
items.hint: Courage is between cowardice and recklessness

items.prompt: ___ wisdom (phronesis) is knowing how to apply virtues in specific situations.
items.hint: Can't be taught by rules — only by experience

description: Compare Utilitarianism vs Deontology on 4 key dimensions.
action: Compare Utilitarianism vs Deontology on 4 key dimensions.
context: Understanding the strengths and weaknesses of each framework helps you use them as tools rather than dogma.
constraint_note: For each pair, think about which side handles that dimension better.

pairs.left: Utilitarianism: Flexible — adapts to context
pairs.right: Deontology: Rigid — same rules regardless of context

pairs.left: Strength: accounts for real-world consequences
pairs.right: Strength: protects individual rights absolutely

pairs.left: Weakness: could justify harming minorities for majority benefit
pairs.right: Weakness: can't handle cases where rules conflict

pairs.left: Example: lie to save a life (good outcome)
pairs.right: Example: never lie, even to a murderer (duty)

description: Peter Singer argues: if you can prevent suffering without sacrificing anything of comparable moral importance, you ought to do it. Do you agree? Why or why not?
action: Peter Singer argues: if you can prevent suffering without sacrificing anything of comparable moral importance, you ought to do it. Do you agree? Why or why not?
context: Singer's argument implies you're morally obligated to donate most of your income to save lives in extreme poverty. It's logically simple but the conclusion is radical.
constraint_note: Engage with the logic. If you disagree, show where the argument breaks down — not just that the conclusion feels extreme.

description: Dilemma: A government surveillance program prevents terrorist attacks but monitors all citizens' communications. Is it justified? Argue both sides.
action: Dilemma: A government surveillance program prevents terrorist attacks but monitors all citizens' communications. Is it justified? Argue both sides.
context: This is a live ethical debate with no consensus. The tension is between collective security and individual privacy.
constraint_note: Write 2-3 sentences FOR surveillance, then 2-3 AGAINST. Then state which argument you find stronger.

description: Learn a 5-step framework for analyzing any ethical dilemma systematically.
action: Learn a 5-step framework for analyzing any ethical dilemma systematically.
context: Most people react to dilemmas with gut feelings. This framework forces structured thinking before reaching a conclusion.
constraint_note: Memorize these steps. You'll use them in future tasks.

steps: Step 1: State the dilemma clearly. What are the competing values or obligations?
Step 2: Identify all stakeholders. Who is affected and how?
Step 3: Apply at least 2 ethical frameworks. What does each recommend?
Step 4: Consider the strongest objection to your initial instinct.
Step 5: State your conclusion with reasons. Acknowledge what you're sacrificing.

description: Do animals have moral rights? If yes, which ones and why? If no, what makes humans special? Argue your position in 4-5 sentences.
action: Do animals have moral rights? If yes, which ones and why? If no, what makes humans special? Argue your position in 4-5 sentences.
context: Peter Singer coined "speciesism" — discrimination based on species. If suffering matters morally, why does the species of the sufferer matter?
constraint_note: Address the strongest counterargument to your position. If you say yes to animal rights, where do you draw the line (insects? bacteria?)? If no, justify the distinction.

description: A self-driving car must choose: swerve to save passengers (killing a pedestrian) or protect the pedestrian (killing passengers). How should it be programmed? Who decides?
action: A self-driving car must choose: swerve to save passengers (killing a pedestrian) or protect the pedestrian (killing passengers). How should it be programmed? Who decides?
context: This is the trolley problem made real by technology. Unlike the thought experiment, someone must actually write the code.
constraint_note: Address: Should the car be utilitarian? Should owners choose? Should governments mandate? What about liability?

description: Was slavery always wrong, even when entire civilizations accepted it? Or can we only judge by the standards of the time? Defend your answer.
action: Was slavery always wrong, even when entire civilizations accepted it? Or can we only judge by the standards of the time? Defend your answer.
context: This question tests whether you believe in moral progress — the idea that humanity can be morally wrong about something and later recognize it.
constraint_note: If you say "always wrong": how do you know which of YOUR current beliefs future generations will condemn? If "judge by the time": does that mean the Holocaust wasn't wrong in Nazi Germany?

description: Learn the anatomy of an argument: premises, conclusions, validity, and soundness.
action: Learn the anatomy of an argument: premises, conclusions, validity, and soundness.
context: Every argument in philosophy, law, science, and daily life has the same structure. Recognizing it lets you evaluate any claim.
constraint_note: Validity = structure is correct. Soundness = structure is correct AND premises are true. A valid argument can have false premises.
example: "All cats can fly. Socrates is a cat. Therefore Socrates can fly." — VALID (structure works) but NOT SOUND (premises are false).

items.reveal: A statement assumed true that supports the conclusion. Arguments need at least one.
items.body: Think of premises as the foundation of a building — the conclusion sits on top. If the foundation is shaky, the whole structure collapses. In everyday arguments, premises are often unstated. 'You should carry an umbrella' has a hidden premise: 'it's going to rain.' Skilled arguers learn to surface these hidden premises because that's usually where arguments are weakest. Next time someone makes a claim, ask: 'what are you assuming is true?'

items.reveal: The claim the argument tries to prove. Should follow logically from premises.
items.body: The conclusion is the destination of the argument — where all the premises are trying to take you. In academic writing, it's usually stated explicitly. In everyday conversation, it's often implied. 'The restaurant has a C health rating and my friend got sick there' — the unstated conclusion is 'don't eat there.' Tip for evaluating arguments: identify the conclusion first, then work backward to find the premises. This prevents you from getting lost in the details.

items.reveal: The conclusion follows logically from the premises. Even if premises are false, the LOGIC works.
items.body: Validity is about STRUCTURE, not truth. 'All fish can fly. My cat is a fish. Therefore my cat can fly.' This argument is perfectly VALID — the conclusion follows logically from the premises. It's just not SOUND because the premises are false. This distinction matters because it separates two different ways an argument can fail: bad logic (invalid) vs. false claims (unsound). You can have true premises and a true conclusion but still be invalid if the logic doesn't connect them properly.

items.reveal: The argument is valid AND all premises are actually true. This is what you want.
items.body: A sound argument is the gold standard: it's valid (the logic works) AND all premises are actually true. 'All humans need oxygen. You are human. Therefore you need oxygen.' Valid structure + true premises = guaranteed true conclusion. In practice, most real-world arguments are hard to prove sound because premises are debatable. 'Democracy is the best system' — is that premise true? Depends on what 'best' means. Recognizing that most arguments are valid-but-debatably-sound is a major step in critical thinking.

description: Learn 5 common logical fallacies — errors in reasoning that make arguments invalid.
action: Learn 5 common logical fallacies — errors in reasoning that make arguments invalid.
context: Fallacies are everywhere: politics, advertising, social media, family arguments. Spotting them is a superpower.
constraint_note: For each fallacy, learn the pattern. Once you see it, you can't unsee it.

items.reveal: "You're wrong because you're young/biased/uneducated." Attacks the arguer instead of the argument.
items.body: Ad hominem means 'against the person.' Instead of addressing someone's argument, you attack their character, motives, or background. 'You can't trust his opinion on climate change — he's funded by oil companies.' The funding IS relevant context, but it doesn't make his argument wrong. Even biased people can make valid arguments. The proper response is to evaluate the argument on its merits, then consider the source as additional context. Politicians use ad hominem constantly because it's emotionally effective even when logically irrelevant.

items.reveal: Misrepresenting someone's position to make it easier to attack. "You want gun control? So you want to ban ALL guns?"
items.body: A straw man is easier to knock down than a real opponent — that's the metaphor. You misrepresent someone's position to make it easier to attack. 'I think we should have some gun regulations.' 'So you want to ban ALL guns and leave people defenseless!' The second person isn't responding to the actual argument — they've built a weaker version and attacked that instead. Straw men are everywhere in political debate. The antidote: before criticizing a position, restate it in the strongest form the other person would accept.

items.reveal: "A famous actor says this diet works." The authority isn't an expert in the relevant field.
items.body: Not all appeals to authority are fallacious — citing a climate scientist on climate change is perfectly reasonable. The fallacy occurs when the authority isn't an expert in the relevant field. A famous actor endorsing a diet, a physicist opining on economics, a CEO giving medical advice — their fame or expertise in one area doesn't transfer. The key question: is this person an expert in THIS specific topic? Even legitimate experts can be wrong, which is why scientific consensus (many experts agreeing) is stronger than any individual authority.

items.reveal: Introducing an irrelevant topic to distract. "Why worry about climate change when there are homeless people?"
items.body: A red herring is a distraction — introducing an irrelevant topic to divert attention from the actual argument. 'Why should we worry about climate change when there are homeless people on our streets?' Homelessness is a real problem, but it has nothing to do with whether climate change is real or requires action. Red herrings work because the new topic is often emotionally compelling. The defense: always ask 'is this relevant to the original question?' If not, name it and redirect.

items.reveal: "You're either with us or against us." Presents only 2 options when more exist.
items.body: A false dilemma presents only two options when more exist. 'You're either with us or against us.' 'Either we ban all immigration or our culture will be destroyed.' Real life almost always has more than two options. The middle ground, the third way, the compromise — these are deliberately excluded to force you into a corner. Politicians love false dilemmas because they simplify complex issues into us-vs-them narratives. Whenever someone presents only two choices, ask: 'what are the other options they're not mentioning?'

description: Learn 5 more logical fallacies. These are subtler and harder to spot.
action: Learn 5 more logical fallacies. These are subtler and harder to spot.
context: These fallacies are especially common in persuasive writing and political speech.
constraint_note: For each one, think of a real example you've encountered recently.

items.reveal: "If we allow X, then Y will happen, then Z." Assumes a chain of consequences without evidence for each link.
items.body: The slippery slope argues that one small step will inevitably lead to extreme consequences. 'If we allow same-sex marriage, next people will want to marry animals.' Each step in the chain MIGHT follow, but the argument assumes they WILL follow without providing evidence for each link. Some slopes are genuinely slippery (addiction often does escalate), but the fallacy occurs when the chain of consequences is asserted without justification. The test: does each step actually follow from the previous one, or is it just fear-mongering?

items.reveal: "God exists because the Bible says so, and the Bible is true because it's God's word." The conclusion is hidden in the premise.
items.body: Circular reasoning uses the conclusion as one of its own premises — it goes in a circle. 'The Bible is true because it's the word of God, and we know it's the word of God because the Bible says so.' The argument assumes what it's trying to prove. A subtler example: 'Free markets are the best economic system because they allow maximum economic freedom.' That's just restating the conclusion in different words. Circular arguments can be hard to spot because they often use different phrasing for the same idea.

items.reveal: Using fear, pity, or anger instead of evidence. "Think of the children!" without showing actual harm.
items.body: This fallacy substitutes emotional manipulation for logical argument. A charity shows heartbreaking photos of suffering children instead of providing data about how effectively donations are used. A politician uses fear of terrorism to justify surveillance without showing it actually prevents attacks. Emotions aren't irrelevant to decision-making — but they should supplement evidence, not replace it. The test: remove the emotional content. Is there still a logical argument underneath? If not, it's pure manipulation.

items.reveal: "Everyone believes this, so it must be true." Popularity doesn't equal truth.
items.body: The bandwagon fallacy assumes that popularity equals truth. 'Millions of people believe in astrology, so there must be something to it.' 'Everyone's investing in crypto, so it must be a good investment.' History is full of things millions of people believed that turned out to be wrong: the earth is flat, bloodletting cures disease, smoking is harmless. Truth isn't determined by vote. The bandwagon is especially dangerous in financial markets, where following the crowd can lead to bubbles and crashes.

items.reveal: "I met two rude people from that city, so everyone there is rude." Drawing broad conclusions from limited data.
items.body: A hasty generalization draws a broad conclusion from too few examples. 'I met two rude people from Paris, so Parisians are rude.' 'My grandfather smoked and lived to 95, so smoking isn't that bad.' The problem is sample size: a few examples can't represent an entire population. This fallacy is the engine of stereotyping — meeting a few members of a group and generalizing to all of them. The antidote: ask 'how many examples am I basing this on?' and 'could there be a selection bias in what I've observed?'

description: Identify the fallacy in each example. Name it.
action: Identify the fallacy in each example. Name it.
context: Spotting fallacies in the wild is the real skill. These examples mimic real arguments.
constraint_note: Read each example carefully and name the specific fallacy.

items.prompt: "You can't trust his opinion on economics — he's a college dropout." This is an ___ fallacy.
items.hint: Attacking the person, not the argument

items.prompt: "If we legalize marijuana, next it'll be cocaine, then heroin." This is a ___ fallacy.
"You either support this policy or you hate freedom." This is a ___ fallacy.
"This is true because it's obviously correct." This is ___ reasoning.
items.hint: The conclusion is hidden in the premise

description: Learn how to construct a valid argument in 4 steps.
action: Learn how to construct a valid argument in 4 steps.
context: Most people state opinions without structure. Learning to build arguments makes your thinking — and your communication — dramatically clearer.
constraint_note: Follow these steps next time you want to convince someone of anything.

steps: Step 1: State your CONCLUSION clearly. What are you trying to prove?
Step 2: Identify your PREMISES. What facts or principles support your conclusion?
Step 3: Check VALIDITY. Does the conclusion actually follow from the premises? Or is there a logical gap?
Step 4: Check SOUNDNESS. Are your premises actually true? Can you provide evidence for each one?

description: Test your understanding of argument fundamentals.
action: Test your understanding of argument fundamentals.
context: These distinctions are the foundation of all logical thinking.
constraint_note: Think carefully — these are tricky.

items.prompt: An argument is ___ if the conclusion follows logically from the premises, even if the premises are false.
An argument is ___ if it is valid AND all premises are true.
___ goes from general rules to specific cases and gives certain conclusions.
items.hint: All humans are mortal → Socrates is mortal

items.prompt: ___ goes from specific observations to general rules and gives probable conclusions.
items.hint: The sun rose every day so far → it will rise tomorrow

description: Find the fallacy: "We should ban violent video games. Since they became popular, youth violence has increased. Clearly, the games cause violence." Name the fallacy and explain why it's wrong.
action: Find the fallacy: "We should ban violent video games. Since they became popular, youth violence has increased. Clearly, the games cause violence." Name the fallacy and explain why it's wrong.
context: Real-world fallacies are harder to spot than textbook examples because they're embedded in plausible-sounding arguments.
constraint_note: Name the specific fallacy, explain why the reasoning is flawed, and suggest what evidence would actually be needed.

description: Construct a valid argument for this conclusion: "Social media does more harm than good." Write 2-3 premises and check if your argument is sound.
action: Construct a valid argument for this conclusion: "Social media does more harm than good." Write 2-3 premises and check if your argument is sound.
context: Building arguments is harder than critiquing them. You need premises that are both true AND logically lead to the conclusion.
constraint_note: Write your premises, then honestly evaluate: are they true? Does the conclusion necessarily follow?

description: Steel-man exercise: take a position you DISAGREE with and argue FOR it as strongly as possible. Pick any controversial topic. Write 3-4 sentences.
action: Steel-man exercise: take a position you DISAGREE with and argue FOR it as strongly as possible. Pick any controversial topic. Write 3-4 sentences.
context: A "steel man" is the opposite of a straw man — you make the opposing argument as STRONG as possible. This is the most important skill in honest thinking.
constraint_note: Choose something you genuinely disagree with. Argue for it so well that someone reading it would think you believe it.

description: Pick a common belief: "Hard work always pays off." Use Socratic questioning to examine it. Write 4-5 questions that expose hidden assumptions.
action: Pick a common belief: "Hard work always pays off." Use Socratic questioning to examine it. Write 4-5 questions that expose hidden assumptions.
context: Socratic questioning doesn't attack a belief — it explores it until the person discovers its limits themselves.
constraint_note: Each question should dig deeper than the last. Don't ask rhetorical questions — ask genuine ones.
example: Example sequence: "What counts as hard work?" → "Pays off how — money, satisfaction, skill?" → "Can you think of someone who worked hard and didn't succeed?" → "What else besides hard work contributed to successes you've seen?"

description: Compare deductive vs inductive arguments with real examples.
action: Compare deductive vs inductive arguments with real examples.
context: Knowing which type of argument you're making (or hearing) tells you how much confidence the conclusion deserves.
constraint_note: Notice: deduction gives certainty but is limited; induction is flexible but never certain.

pairs.left: Deductive: All mammals breathe air. Whales are mammals. Therefore whales breathe air.
pairs.right: Inductive: Every whale we've observed breathes air. Therefore all whales probably breathe air.

pairs.left: Certainty: if premises are true, conclusion MUST be true
pairs.right: Probability: conclusion is LIKELY but could be wrong

pairs.left: Risk: premises might be wrong or too narrow
pairs.right: Risk: new evidence could overturn the conclusion

pairs.left: Used in: math, logic, law
pairs.right: Used in: science, everyday reasoning, predictions

description: Identify the fallacy in each real-world example.
action: Identify the fallacy in each real-world example.
context: These are modeled on actual arguments you'd encounter in news, social media, and conversations.
constraint_note: Name the specific fallacy for each.

items.prompt: "90% of people surveyed agree this product is the best." This is a ___ fallacy.
"How can you worry about animal rights when children are starving?" This is a ___.
items.hint: Changing the subject to something irrelevant

items.prompt: "My friend went to Paris and got robbed. Paris is dangerous." This is a ___.
"Look at this photo of a suffering child. You MUST support our cause." This is an ___.

description: Argument mapping: take this claim and break it into premises. "Democracy is the best form of government." Write 3 premises that would support this, then identify the weakest one.
action: Argument mapping: take this claim and break it into premises. "Democracy is the best form of government." Write 3 premises that would support this, then identify the weakest one.
context: Argument mapping makes invisible reasoning visible. Most people hold conclusions without knowing what premises they're relying on.
constraint_note: Write 3 premises, label each as strong or weak, and explain why the weakest one is vulnerable.

description: The Liar Paradox: "This statement is false." If it's true, then it's false. If it's false, then it's true. Is this a real problem for logic, or just a word game? Argue your position.
action: The Liar Paradox: "This statement is false." If it's true, then it's false. If it's false, then it's true. Is this a real problem for logic, or just a word game? Argue your position.
context: Paradoxes aren't just puzzles — they reveal limits in our logical systems. The Liar Paradox contributed to Gödel's incompleteness theorems.
constraint_note: Don't just describe the paradox — take a position on whether it matters and why.

description: Deconstruct this argument: "AI will take all jobs → mass unemployment → social collapse. Therefore we must ban AI development." Identify every logical gap.
action: Deconstruct this argument: "AI will take all jobs → mass unemployment → social collapse. Therefore we must ban AI development." Identify every logical gap.
context: Complex real-world arguments often chain multiple claims together. Each link in the chain needs separate evaluation.
constraint_note: For each step in the chain, ask: is this necessarily true? What evidence would you need? What alternatives exist?

description: Is it ethical to use emotional persuasion (rhetoric) when you believe your cause is just? Or should arguments always be purely logical? Defend your position.
